hub.title: Freight Planning Library
hub.description: Agency, product, and decision guides for importers, exporters, manufacturers, and buyers preparing freight before they request a quote.

sources.name: USDA APHIS - Plant and Plant Product Imports
International Trade Administration - Foreign-Trade Zones Board

pages.title: CBP importer of record freight guide
pages.description: How importer of record details affect freight planning, customs review, entry records, broker coordination, and shipment timing.

sections.heading: What to clarify before freight moves
items: Identify the importer of record before pickup, not after arrival.
Confirm the importer number, broker instructions, buyer and seller records, and invoice details align.
If CBP Form 5106 is needed, start that process before the freight plan depends on release timing.

sections.heading: Why freight teams care
items: A shipment can be physically ready but commercially blocked by incomplete importer identity details.
Broker questions can delay truck, air, or ocean handoff if the right party is not available.
Importer data should match the commercial invoice, purchase record, and receiver expectation.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: State whether the importer is already established.
Share broker contact status if available.
Flag first-time importer, new product line, or unfamiliar supplier situations early.

pages.title: HTS and HS code import planning guide
pages.description: How HTS and HS code context helps importers prepare documents, product descriptions, duty context, and broker questions before freight moves.

sections.heading: What to prepare
items: Use product identity, material, function, composition, and end use to support classification review.
Keep the commercial invoice description specific enough for the broker and importer to understand the product.
Do not treat a freight quote as classification advice; use the correct professional or agency resources where needed.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Weak descriptions create extra questions before clearance.
HTS context can affect documents, duties, admissibility, and product-specific agency review.
Classification uncertainty should be handled before urgent cargo is dispatched.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: Share known HTS or HS context if available.
Explain product use and material composition in plain language.
Flag regulated products, samples, warranty returns, and mixed-product shipments.

pages.title: FDA regulated products import checklist
pages.description: A freight planning checklist for FDA-regulated products, including food, cosmetics, medical devices, drugs, and radiation-emitting electronic products.

sections.heading: What to identify
items: Confirm whether the product may fall under FDA review before pickup.
Separate food, cosmetics, medical devices, drugs, tobacco, animal feed, and radiation-emitting electronic products.
Prepare manufacturer, product, label, intended use, quantity, and importer information.

sections.heading: Why it matters
items: FDA-regulated goods may need additional review before release.
A shipment may be delayed if product identity, labels, or importer context are unclear.
Document readiness affects route timing, warehouse planning, and receiver expectations.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: State the product category and intended use.
Flag samples, returns, repairs, or low-value shipments.
Share label status, product codes, or prior import history if available.

pages.title: Medical device import freight checklist
pages.description: Freight planning notes for medical devices and radiation-emitting electronic products before shipment, routing, and customs review.

sections.heading: What to confirm
items: Identify whether the cargo is a medical device, component, accessory, sample, return, or repair.
Keep model numbers, serial numbers, manufacturer details, labels, and intended use clear.
Separate new commercial sale shipments from warranty, repair, exhibition, or evaluation shipments.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Device identity can affect FDA review and entry data.
Serial and model detail can reduce confusion between invoice, packing list, and receiver records.
Sensitive devices may also need packaging, staging, condition photos, or white glove handling.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: Share device category and shipment purpose.
Flag if cargo includes batteries or radiation-emitting electronics.
Confirm whether the receiver needs appointment, inspection, or special handling.

pages.title: FCC electronics import checklist
pages.description: A practical checklist for importers moving electronics, radio-frequency devices, samples, components, and connected equipment.

sections.heading: What to clarify
items: Identify whether the product emits radio-frequency energy or includes wireless functionality.
Separate commercial sale goods from samples, testing units, returns, and product-development shipments.
Keep model numbers, FCC IDs if available, manufacturer details, and battery status organized.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Electronics shipments often need more precise descriptions than generic 'devices' or 'parts'.
Samples and commercial shipments may require different supporting context.
Battery status, packaging, and product identity affect carrier acceptance and routing.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: State product function and whether wireless capability is present.
Flag batteries, prototypes, samples, or warranty returns.
Share model, serial, SKU, and receiver inspection needs.

pages.title: CPSC consumer products import checklist
pages.description: How consumer product safety context can affect freight documents, importer preparation, certificate questions, and shipment timing.

sections.heading: What to identify
items: Confirm whether the product is a consumer product that may be regulated by CPSC.
Keep product description, age grading, labels, certificates, manufacturer, importer, and SKU details organized.
Separate toys, children’s products, electronics, furniture, and mixed consumer goods where requirements may differ.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Safety certificate or product identity questions can slow release.
Generic invoice descriptions can create avoidable review loops.
Retail deadlines should not be planned before document readiness is clear.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: Share product type, intended user, and retail channel.
Flag children’s products and regulated consumer goods early.
Confirm whether certificates or test documents are already available.

pages.title: USDA agriculture import checklist
pages.description: Freight planning notes for plant, plant product, animal product, and agricultural commodity imports before routing and pickup.

sections.heading: What to identify
items: Confirm whether the cargo is a plant, plant product, animal product, food ingredient, sample, or agricultural commodity.
Check whether permits, phytosanitary documents, treatment records, or inspection expectations may apply.
Keep origin, species or commodity details, packaging, and processing status clear.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Agricultural cargo can be delayed by missing permit or treatment context.
Temperature, inspection, and receiver timing can matter as much as the freight lane.
Cargo description should be specific enough for broker and agency review.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: Share commodity name, origin country, processing status, and packaging.
Flag live plants, seeds, wood products, animal-derived goods, or temperature-sensitive cargo.
Confirm if permits or certificates have already been issued.

pages.title: Lithium battery freight checklist
pages.description: A practical freight checklist for lithium batteries, battery-powered devices, samples, returns, repairs, and electronics shipments.

sections.heading: What to confirm
items: Identify whether batteries are alone, packed with equipment, installed in equipment, damaged, defective, or returns.
Confirm battery chemistry, watt-hour or lithium content context, quantity, packaging, labels, and transport mode.
Separate new sale goods from repair, warranty, sample, and damaged battery shipments.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Battery status can affect carrier acceptance, air eligibility, packaging, labeling, and documentation.
A shipment can be delayed if the battery configuration is discovered after pickup.
Electronics freight plans should include battery questions before routing is quoted.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: State battery configuration in plain language.
Flag damaged, defective, recalled, or return batteries immediately.
Share product photos, labels, safety documents, and receiver requirements if available.

pages.title: DDP vs DAP freight planning guide
pages.description: How DDP and DAP trade term choices affect freight planning, responsibility, destination charges, customs expectations, and buyer communication.

sections.heading: What to clarify
items: Confirm the trade term and named place before freight is quoted.
Decide who owns import customs, duties, taxes, destination charges, and final delivery risk.
Align the invoice, purchase order, and buyer expectations before cargo leaves origin.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: DDP and DAP can create very different responsibilities at destination.
Unclear terms can produce disputes over charges, delivery attempts, and customs work.
The freight plan should match the commercial agreement, not only the route.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: Share the trade term and named place.
State who is importer of record if known.
Flag whether the buyer expects delivered cost, delivery only, or customs-inclusive support.

pages.title: Bonded warehouse vs FTZ guide
pages.description: A planning comparison for bonded warehouse and Foreign-Trade Zone discussions before freight, storage, customs, or distribution decisions.

sections.heading: What to compare
items: Clarify whether goods need storage, manipulation, export, distribution, duty deferral, or staged release.
Confirm who controls the facility, customs status, inventory records, and release process.
Separate ordinary warehousing from customs-controlled storage or FTZ program questions.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: The storage choice can affect inland routing, documentation, release timing, and final delivery planning.
Cargo may need stricter inventory and status control than a normal warehouse move.
Freight timing should reflect customs-controlled handling, not only transportation speed.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: State why goods need staged storage.
Flag re-export, delayed duty, assembly, kitting, inspection, or distribution needs.
Confirm if a customs broker or FTZ operator is already involved.

pages.title: Landed cost freight checklist
pages.description: A practical checklist for building landed cost context before comparing freight options, supplier terms, duties, taxes, and destination charges.

sections.heading: What to include
items: Product value, freight, insurance, duties, taxes, destination handling, storage, delivery, and service fees.
Trade term, named place, importer responsibility, and broker status.
Product classification context and any agency review risk.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: The lowest freight quote may not produce the best landed cost outcome.
Destination charges, storage, re-delivery, and document delays can change the total cost picture.
Landed cost needs commercial, customs, and freight information together.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: Share the supplier terms and destination expectation.
Flag whether duty/tax estimates are already available.
Separate urgent replacement cargo from planned replenishment cargo.

pages.title: FDA vs CPSC import review guide
pages.description: A comparison page for importers deciding whether FDA, CPSC, or both may matter before moving consumer goods, devices, cosmetics, or regulated products.

sections.heading: What to separate
items: FDA context can matter for food, cosmetics, drugs, medical devices, tobacco, animal feed, and radiation-emitting products.
CPSC context can matter for consumer products and hazardous substances offered for import.
Some products may raise more than one agency question depending on intended use and product design.

sections.heading: Freight impact
items: Agency questions affect document readiness, broker review, and release timing.
Product descriptions should not hide intended use, labels, age grading, or device function.
Mixed shipments should separate product categories before routing.

sections.heading: Useful inquiry notes
items: State product category, intended user, intended use, and retail channel.
Flag cosmetics, medical devices, children’s products, electronics, and samples.
Share label, certificate, product code, or prior import context if available.
